data.title: UnitedHealth seeks immediate repayment of post-hack relief funds: WSJ
data.description:
UnitedHealth Group (NYSE:UNH) is seeking an immediate repayment of loans it made to various healthcare establishments affected by the cyberattack on its Change Healthcare unit in 2024, The Wall Street Journal reported on Friday.

Citing letters sent by the managed care firm’s Optum unit, the Journal reported that UnitedHealth (NYSE:UNH) has demanded debtors repay the full loan amount or risk facing offsets against their insurance claims. In many cases, the company has sought repayments within days.

According to the Eden Prairie, Minnesota-based firm, it had channeled roughly $8.9B to businesses after Change, which handled about 50% of the nation’s medical claims, faced a cyberattack in February 2024, crippling the U.S. healthcare system.

While the company had recovered about $3.2B of those funds as of October, its latest demands contrast with reassurances CEO Andrew Witty made before the Senate Finance Committee last year.

During a Senate hearing in May, Witty said that the healthcare providers affected by the hack will have 45 days to repay the funds when their cash flows return to normal and their claim backlogs get fully cleared.  

A spokesperson for Change Healthcare confirmed that the company has begun to recoup the funds. "We continue to work with providers on repayment and other options and continue to reach out to those providers that have not been responsive to previous calls or email requests for more information," the spokesman added.

data.title: Wells Fargo outlines 2025 net interest income growth range amid strategic investments
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Wells Fargo (WFC) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Charlie Scharf highlighted a 16% year-over-year increase in diluted earnings per share, driven by improved fee-based revenue and disciplined expense management. He emphasized significant progress in risk and control work, including the termination of five consent orders in the quarter.
  • Management reported $4.8 billion of capital returned to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases, reducing average common shares outstanding by 8% year-over-year.
  • The company launched a partnership with Volkswagen and Audi for preferred purchase financing in the U.S., marking a strategic step in growing its auto business.
  • Scharf announced leadership changes in the Cards and Merchant Services division, with Ed Olebe joining as the new head, succeeding Ray Fischer.
  • CFO Mike Santomassimo reported net income of $4.9 billion or $1.39 per diluted share. He detailed a $313 million tax benefit and a $263 million gain from the sale of a segment in the commercial mortgage servicing business, partially offset by $149 million in losses related to investment portfolio repositioning.

Outlook

  • Management expects full-year 2025 net interest income to grow by 1% to 3% compared to 2024, with more growth anticipated in the second half of the year. Santomassimo noted that the outcome will depend on factors like rates, deposit flows, and loan demand.
  • Noninterest expense is forecasted at approximately $54.2 billion for 2025, reflecting ongoing efficiency initiatives and increased investment in technology and other areas.
  • The company plans to maintain a capital position well above regulatory minimums and continue share repurchases based on business opportunities and economic conditions.

Financial Results

  • Net interest income decreased 3% from the previous quarter due to fewer days in the period and lower rates on floating rate assets.
  • Fee-based revenue saw growth across multiple business lines, aiding diversification away from net interest income.
  • Noninterest expense declined 3% year-over-year, attributed to lower operating losses and efficiency improvements.
  • Credit performance improved, with net charge-offs declining to 45 basis points of average loans, driven by better commercial portfolio results.
  • Average loans showed slight growth, marking the first increase since Q1 2023, with strength in commercial and industrial loans.

Q&A

  • Scott Siefers, Piper Sandler, inquired about customer sentiment amid economic uncertainty. Scharf noted continued strength in behavior but a cautious outlook among clients.
  • Ken Usdin, Autonomous Research, asked about regulatory progress and its impact. Scharf expressed confidence in the objectivity of regulators and the bank’s ability to meet expectations.
  • John McDonald, Truist Securities, sought details on commercial loan growth. Santomassimo attributed it to increased utilization and new client acquisitions in asset-based lending and mid-corporate segments.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were cautiously optimistic, focusing on regulatory progress and economic outlook uncertainties. Questions emphasized clarity on net interest income and credit quality.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, highlighting strategic advancements and operational improvements. Prepared remarks conveyed a proactive stance, while Q&A responses demonstrated readiness to adapt to evolving conditions.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance remained consistent with prior expectations, though management indicated a shift toward the lower end of net interest income growth projections.
  • Fee-based revenue growth continued to offset declines in net interest income, reflecting diversification efforts.
  • Regulatory progress accelerated with the termination of five consent orders, compared to no major closures in the previous quarter.
  • Leadership changes in key segments, such as Cards and Merchant Services, were significant updates not present in Q4 2024.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged the potential for economic volatility and slower growth in 2025, citing policy changes and market uncertainties.
  • Credit conditions were closely monitored, with focus on commercial real estate and consumer credit stability.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the sustainability of net interest income growth amid rate and loan demand fluctuations.

Final Takeaway

Wells Fargo demonstrated solid progress in Q1 2025, with improvements in fee-based revenue, expense management, and credit performance. Regulatory milestones and strategic partnerships, such as the Volkswagen and Audi financing agreement, signal a focus on long-term growth. Management’s cautious yet optimistic outlook for net interest income and continued investment in technology and operational efficiency underpin its strategy for navigating economic uncertainties.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Catalyst Watch: Meta takes on the FTC, Taiwan Semi earnings, and Amazon satellites take flight
data.description:
Welcome to Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch - a breakdown of some of next week's actionable events that stand out. Read more about the events that may impact stock prices next week.

 

Monday - April 14

 

  • Volatility watch - Azul S.A. (AZUL) and Strategy (MSTR) have a high level of implied volatility based on options trading. Hertz Global Holdings (HTZ) and PureCycle Technologies (PCT) have an elevated level of short interest outstanding on them heading into the new week. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Chimerix (CMRX), OptiNose (OPTN), and First Capital (FCAP). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Open Lending (LPRO), Curis (CRIS), and Conduent (CNDT). Analysts have also circled Aurora Innovation (AUR) as a stock to watch, with the autonomous driving tech company closing in on a commercial driverless launch in Texas.
  • Tariff check - A baseline 10% tariff on all imports into the U.S. began on April 5. China faces much higher tariff rates.
  • IPO watch - Haoxin Holdings (HXHX), Chagee Holdings (CHA), Airo Group (AIRO), OMS Energy Technologies (OMSE), and Jyong Biotech (MENS) are all expected to price their IPOs and begin trading during the week.
  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Goldman Sachs (GS) and M&T Bank (MTB).
  • 12:00 p.m. Richmond Federal Reserve Bank President Thomas Barkin will speak on "Driving Through the Economic Fog" before the Greater Greer Chamber of Commerce.
  • All day - Notable investor events include HP's (HPQ) annual meeting, Ionis Pharmaceuticals' (IONS) investor day.
  • All day - The tender offer for QXO (QXO) to acquire Beacon Roofing (BECN) expires. Beacon Roofing traded about 1.2% below the deal price at the time of publication.
  • All day - OPEC will publish its monthly oil market report.
  • All day - A United Launch Alliance Atlas 5 rocket is scheduled to launch a batch of 27 of Amazon's (AMZN) Project Kuiper internet satellites, which will be the first production satellites for Amazon's satellite internet megaconstellation.
  • All day - Meta Platforms (META) will face off against the U.S. Federal Trade Commission in a high-stakes antitrust trial that could lead to Instagram and WhatsApp being divested.
  • 7:40 p.m. Atlanta Federal Reserve Bank President Raphael Bostic speaks on monetary policy in a moderated conversation hosted by Emory University.

 

Tuesday - April 15

 

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Albertsons (ACI), Bank of America (BAC), Citigroup (C), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), PNC Financial (PNC), and United Airlines (UAL). Options trading implies a share price swing of 8% for JB Hunt (JBHT) after its report is released.
  • All day - The three-day Salesforce (CRM) Tableau Conference will begin.
  • 9:40 a.m. General Motors (GM) will participate in the Bank of America Securities Auto Summit.

 

Wednesday - April 16

 

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Abbott Laboratories (ABT), U.S. Bancorp (USB), Travelers (TRV), Kinder Morgan (KMI), and CSX (CSX). Options trading implies a double-digit share price swing for Alcoa (AA) after its report.
  • All day - Subaru (OTCPK:FUJHY), Hyundai (OTCPK:HYMTF), Maserati (STLA), Toyota (TM), and AB Volvo's (OTCPK:VOLAF) Mack Trucks will hold press events at the New York International Auto Show.
  • All day - DocuSign (DOCU) will hold its Momentum NYC event. CEO Allan Thygesen will give a keynote speech. Last year, DocuSign rallied after it showcased its new platform at the event.
  • All day - Daktronics (DAKT) will hold a special shareholder meeting to vote on a reincorporation proposal and other matters. The reincorporation vote is part of a broader agreement between Daktronics and Alta Fox, which included a standstill agreement and Alta Fox agreeing to support the move to Delaware from South Dakota.
  • 8:30 a.m. The U.S. Census Bureau will release the March Retail Sales report.
  • 9:40 a.m. Ford Motor (F) Vice Chair John Lawler will participate in the Bank of America Securities Auto Summit. Lawler will join analyst John Murphy for a fireside chat to discuss how the Ford+ plan is remaking Ford into a higher growth, higher margin, more capital efficient company.
  • 12:00 p.m. Cleveland Federal Reserve Bank President Beth Hammack will speak on "Fed 101" and participate in a moderated question-and-answer session at an event in Columbus.
  • 4:50 p.m. Rivian Automotive (RIVN) CFO Claire McDonough will participate in the Bank of America Securities 2025 Automotive Summit.

 

Thursday - April 17

 

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Taiwan Semi (TSM), UnitedHealth Group (UNH), Netflix (NFLX), American Express (AXP), Ally Financial (ALLY), and Charles Schwab (SCHW).
  • All day - Shareholders with Playa Hotels & Resorts (PLYA) will vote on the proposed merger with Hyatt Hotels (H).
  • All day - President Trump and Italian Prime Minister Meloni are scheduled to meet for tariff talks.
  • 7:45 a.m. The European Central Bank will announce its rate decision.
  • 8:30 p.m. European Central Bank President Christine Lagarde will hold a press conference.

 

Friday - April 18

 

  • All day - The U.S. stock markets will be closed for the Good Friday holiday.
  • All day - It is the FDA action date for Sanofi's (SNY) Dupixent for Chronic spontaneous urticaria.

Seeking Alpha Editor Josh Fineman contributed to this story.

data.title: Marijuana rescheduling process remains on hold, DEA says
data.description:
• The U.S. Drug Enforcement Agency communicated to an in-house judge on Thursday that a process to reschedule marijuana initiated under the Biden Administration remains on hold, with no briefings currently scheduled.
  • DEA’s update comes as the agency marks three months since the DEA Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) John Mulrooney’s decision to pause the process in January to consider a legal challenge filed by several pro-rescheduling parties.
  • A hearing scheduled for Jan. 21 on marijuana rescheduling was canceled after Mulrooney allowed pro-reformists to file an interlocutory appeal.
  • “To date, Movants’ interlocutory appeal to the Acting Administrator regarding their Motion to Reconsider remains pending with the Acting Administrator,” DEA attorneys stated in a joint status report on Thursday. “No briefing schedule has been set.”

data.title: Huntington Ingalls, L3Harris surge as Goldman double-upgrades to Buy from Sell
data.description:
Huntington Ingalls (NYSE:HII) +7.1% and L3Harris Technologies (NYSE:LHX) +4% in Friday's trading as Goldman Sachs double upgrades the defense contractors to Buy from Sell with $234 and $263 price targets, respectively, raised from $145 and $198, as the bank becomes "less cautious on the sector, as the medium-term defense budget picture looks better than we thought."

The U.S. Department of Defense budget is "still likely closer to a peak than a trough, and it is not entirely clear where each of the next few years will land, plus there are still margin questions. But at a minimum the near-term trajectory is looking higher than we thought, for a group of stocks that had moved relatively out of favor," Goldman analyst Noah Poponak says, also upgrading Northrop Grumman (NOC) to Neutral from Sell but downgrading Booz Allen Hamilton (BAH)‌ to Neutral from Buy.

The Trump administration has said it is planning for a FY 2026 DoD budget near $1T - "while that lacks total clarity around definition and will need to make its way through Congress, the total drives a high growth rate, whereas a decline seemed possible given total government spending reduction efforts, prior comments, and already heightened levels," Poponak writes.

data.title: Stocks jump amid bond selloff, as Wall Street looks to close wild week on a high
data.description:
Wall Street on Friday overcame a volatile morning trading session to push higher, looking to end a wild week on a positive note. A White House official said "very good progress" had been made on trade negotiations, and that the "phones have been ringing off the hook" due to more than 75 countries calling in to make deals.

Equities also got a boost from a bruising selloff in the bond market, which sent yields soaring.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +1.42% at 5,342.62 points in late afternoon trade. The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +1.62% at 16,652.15 points, while the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was +1.33% at 40,118.71 points.  

White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said the U.S. had heard from Israel, South Korea, Japan, Vietnam and Italy on trade negotiations. She also said that the tariff rate on China would remain at 145%, which means the U.S. is - for now - not retaliating to China's raising of their tariff rate on American imports to 125% earlier in the day.    

Market participants also digested a host of quarterly earnings from major banks and more inflation data.

Before the opening bell, investors received earnings from the number one and number three U.S. banks by assets: JPMorgan (JPM) +3.4%, and Wells Fargo (WFC) -1.2%, respectively. The lenders delivered Y/Y increases in Q1 net income, while the CEOs of both firms issued cautionary remarks about tariffs. 

Also reporting results were Morgan Stanley (MS) +1.1%, and asset manager BlackRock (BLK) +2.5%.

Turning to the economic calendar, traders were treated to another favorable inflation report following Thursday's update on consumer prices. The core producer price index (PPI) for March unexpectedly came in at -0.1% M/M, compared to a consensus estimate of +0.1%.

Additionally, the University of Michigan's gauge of consumer sentiment for April plunged 11% from March. Year-ahead inflation expectations jumped to their highest level since 1981.     

Over in the fixed-income markets, a bruising bond selloff in longer-term maturities extended to a fourth day in five. The benchmark U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) hit a two-month-high, and was last up 8 basis points to 4.47%. The shorter-end more rate-sensitive 2-year yield (US2Y) was up 10 basis points to 3.95%.

For more, see how Treasury yields have done across the curve on the Seeking Alpha bond page.

"Investors are keeping a close eye on the bond market where there’s still evidence of stress with high-yield credit spreads widening dramatically. Last week yields tumbled, and bond prices soared, following President Trump’s announcement of reciprocal tariffs. This looked like a ‘flight to safety’ trade as U.S. Treasuries are viewed as the safest asset available," David Morrison, senior market analyst at Trade Nation, said. 

"But yields have soared this week ... Market insiders are still unsure of the reason for this turnaround as the jump in yields was one of the sharpest moves in twenty years. But as yields show signs of settling at higher levels, it looks as if the market is primed for higher inflation thanks to tariffs, rather than slowing economic growth. In addition, the prevailing view is that it was these bond market moves which persuaded President Trump to row back on his extensive tariff measures," Morrison added.

Trump earlier this week on Wednesday issued a 90-day pause on most reciprocal tariffs except for those against China. The announcement sparked one of the most explosive intraday rallies in Wall Street history, sending the S&P 500 (SP500) to its best day since the 2008 financial crisis and the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) to its second-best day ever.

Thanks to that rally, the S&P (SP500) on Friday was also on track for its best weekly advance since early November last year.

data.title: Bitcoin set to stay flat week on week as tariff turmoil persists
data.description:
Bitcoin (BTC-USD) is on course to stay largely flat on a weekly basis in a period where tariff talks dominated the headlines.

The world’s largest cryptocurrency suffered a significant sell-off in early trade on Monday, with bitcoin sliding below the $75,000 mark for the first time since Nov. 7 last year. BTC, however, recovered some of its momentum later in the same day.

Bitcoin’s struggle continued on Tuesday, as the cryptocurrency that weathered the initial reciprocal tariff response seemed to falter.

The crypto market, however, jolted higher Wednesday after President Donald Trump announced a 90-day pause on tit-for-tat tariffs for more than 75 countries but a higher levy on Chinese imports. BTC regained the $80K threshold on the day.

Stoking the gains on Wednesday, Trump announced a 90-day pause on counter tariffs for more than 75 countries that had reached out for negotiations, as well as a reduced 10% counter tariff rate for all countries but China during the span.

“Following Trump's 90-day tariff pause, the market experienced a notable rally, with Bitcoin surging to a key level just below $84,000. This upswing was fueled by optimism that the worst may be behind us,” said Jupiter Zheng, Partner, Liquid Fund and Research, HashKey Capital

BTC also traded above the $80K mark for most of Thursday, but failed to stay above the threshold as U.S.- China trade war seemed to escalate.

While potential headwinds remain, such as retaliatory tariffs from China in response to Trump's 125% increase, the start of negotiations with other countries offers some hope. As U.S. regulators continue to streamline regulatory hurdles and implement more favorable policies, it's possible that bitcoin and other cryptocurrencies have reached a bottom, assuming no unexpected surprises emerge. The industry may not have fully priced in these developments, leaving room for potential growth, Zheng added.

“The market is rallying in response to anticipation that most trading partners will negotiate trade deals with US, avoiding a full-fledged trade war. That being said, continued tariffs against China and vice versa will lead to a realignment of global trade that could drastically change how the world operates. We remain cautious until we see the consequences of this play out over the coming months,” Jeff Mei, COO at BTSE said.

On Friday, China has announced an increase in tariffs on U.S. goods to 125%, up from the previous rate of 84%, effective April 12, and stated it will disregard any additional tariff hikes announced by Washington moving forward. BTC, however, did not seem to react much to the news.

In the early days of the week, the digital currency was under pressure by widespread bearish sentiment across the crypto market and ongoing struggles in U.S. stocks amid Trump’s aggressive tariff policies.

“If bitcoin is in a period of weakness, investors seeking safety and stability might turn to gold,” CME Group said.

“The recent divergence between gold and bitcoin highlights the complex interplay of economic, political and market forces,” CME Group added.

Notable News

  • Argentina's lower house of Congress opted to launch a probe into the Libra cryptocurrency, which President Javier Milei hyped before its sudden collapse cost investors hundreds of millions of dollars, according to a recent release.
  • BitFuFu (FUFU) announced on Wednesday bitcoin holdings of 1,847 as of March 31, an increase of 47 BTC from the end of February.
  • Bitdeer Technologies (BTDR) said in March its self-mined bitcoin (BTC-USD) came in at 114 BTC, up from 110 BTC it had mined in the prior month but down from 294 BTC in the same period the prior year.
  • Interactive Brokers (IBKR) added more cryptocurrencies to its trading platform, including Chainlink (LINK-USD), Avalanche (AVAX-USD) and Sui (SUI-USD)
  • Standard Chartered predicted Tuesday that the XRP (XRP-USD) cryptocurrency could skyrocket more than tenfold to $12.50 per coin before President Donald Trump's term expires on Jan. 20, 2029.
  • Ripple Labs, the company behind the XRP (XRP-USD) cryptocurrency, agreed to buy multi-asset prime brokerage firm Hidden Road for $1.25B, a deal that's expected to bolster Ripple's efforts on the stablecoin front, it said.
  • The U.S. Justice Department recently notified employees that the regulator was scrapping a unit focused on crypto enforcement, aligning with the Trump administration's push for deregulation.
  • Strategy (MSTR) recognized a $5.91B unrealized loss on its digital assets for Q1 2025, which is expected to result in a net loss for the quarter, the company said on Monday.

Crypto Moves

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) up 5.6% to $84K as of 2:08 pm on Friday. Ether (ETH-USD) was up 4.3% to $1.6K, while DOGE (DOGE-USD) up 5.6%.

 

 

"Bitcoin, launched in 2009 as a payment system, pivoted to a reserve asset narrative after the 2018 BTC Cash Fork. A 'Trumpcession' may see it finally mature into a reserve," Seeking Alpha analyst Geneva Investor said.

While Bitcoin has not yet matured into a global reserve asset and remains correlated with risk assets, its superior technical qualities make it a strong long-term buy, in my view, Geneva Investor added.

data.title: Analog Devices, Texas Instruments are Citi's top chip stocks ahead of potential downturn
data.description:
Investment firm Citi said Analog Devices (NASDAQ:ADI) and Texas Instruments (NASDAQ:TXN) are now its top picks in the semiconductor space ahead of a potential downturn, citing their “defensive nature.”

“High-end analog companies such as ADI and TXN historically outperformed during downturns and we would expect the same to occur amid our expected recession,” analyst Christopher Danely wrote in a note to clients. “With sales and units down roughly 25% from the peak, we believe the analog space should be relatively resilient in the event of a recession, given healthier inventory dynamics.”

Danely has a Buy rating on Analog Devices and Texas Instruments and price targets of $235 and $210, respectively.

Looking at earnings season, Danely said there should be “decent” guidance coming from companies, but companies could also lower their guidance on average by 10% as they digest the impact of tariffs. “Given our expectation of a recession, ADI and TXN are now our top picks, due to their outperformance in recessions,” Danely added.

Aside from Analog Devices and TI, Broadcom (AVGO) is the firm's third top pick (also with a Buy rating), as Danely said he expects its guidance to be cut less than the average. 

On the flip side, Danely cut his estimates the most for ON Semiconductor (ON), Intel (INTC) and GlobalFoundries (GFS) and expects companies like ON, GlobalFoundries and Micron (MU) to be the most at risk, given their low margin profiles.

data.title: Gold heads for its strongest three-day performance since COVID-19 pandemic
data.description:
Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) prices surged higher on Friday, marking a significant move for the precious metal as it heads for its strongest three-day performance since March 2020—when markets were reeling from the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic.

As of Friday afternoon, gold is trading near $3,25/oz, up 2.5% on the day and breaking through its previous high of $3,201 set back on April 2. Over the past three sessions, the metal has rallied 8.6% from its April 9 opening price of$2,998/oz.

 Investors are flocking to gold amid growing economic uncertainty, with recession fears intensifying and renewed concerns over global tariffs adding to the market’s unease. The broader trend remains firmly bullish as gold has soared 23.3% so far in 2025.

As the demand for safe-haven assets accelerates, gold continues to shine as a top performer in a volatile financial landscape. 

See the below chart that Bespoke Investment Group provided:

For investors looking to jeep a closer pulse on the price of gold they can look towards both bold backed and gold miner exchange traded funds.

 

 

Gold ETFs: (NYSEARCA:IAU), (NYSEARCA:SGOL), (NYSEARCA:GLDM), (NYSEARCA:IAUM), (NYSEARCA:OUNZ), (NYSEARCA:BAR), (NYSEARCA:UGL), (BATS:AAAU), (NYSEARCA:PHYS), (NYSEARCA:GDX), (NYSEARCA:GDXJ), (NYSEARCA:NUGT), (NASDAQ:RING), (NYSEARCA:SGDM), (NYSEARCA:GOAU), (NYSEARCA:GDXU), and (NYSEARCA:DUST).

data.title: General Motors to idle Ingersoll, Ontario assembly plant - report
data.description:
• General Motors (NYSE:GM) employees at the company’s Ingersoll, Ontario plant have reportedly been told the facility will close for three weeks, reopening briefly, and then shut down again until October.
  • According to sources cited by CTV News, management plans to “purify” the workforce, keeping only essential employees needed to run one shift.
  • Employees were notified of the shutdown on Friday.
  • While it’s unclear if the closure is directly related to U.S. trade policy, General Motors (NYSE:GM) has a heavy exposure to U.S. tariffs on imported products from Canada with two major plants in the country, including the CAMI Assembly plant in Ingersoll.
  • GM (NYSE:GM) shares were trading lower for the sixth time in 7 days, and set to close in the red for a third consecutive week.
